stage.name: Cryptocurrency Guide
stage.description: UTXO model, secp256k1 elliptic-curve cryptography, mining economics, altcoins, and on-chain analysis.

elements.content: Bitcoin: The UTXO Model
Unspent Transaction Outputs — the ledger model powering Bitcoin

21M BTC
Supply

~180M
UTXOs

UTXO Basics

Every Bitcoin tx consumes existing UTXOs as inputs and creates new UTXOs as outputs

Your 'balance' = sum of all UTXOs locked to your public key — there is no account balance

Coinbase tx (first in every block) creates new BTC from nothing — only source of new supply

Change UTXO: if you spend 1 BTC but owe 0.3 BTC, you receive a 0.7 BTC UTXO back to yourself

Transaction Anatomy

Input: UTXO reference (txid + vout index) + scriptSig (unlocking script / signature)

Output: value in satoshis + scriptPubKey (locking script — usually P2PKH or P2WPKH)

Transaction fee = sum(inputs) - sum(outputs) — miners collect all fees in a block

SegWit (BIP141): moves witness data outside base tx, reducing fee weight by ~40%

Script & Locking

Bitcoin Script is a stack-based language — intentionally NOT Turing-complete

P2PKH: Pay-to-Public-Key-Hash — most common, requires sig + pubkey to unlock

P2SH: Pay-to-Script-Hash — enables multisig; redeem script revealed at spend time

P2TR (Taproot): Schnorr + MAST — multisig looks identical to single-sig on-chain
Elliptic Curve Cryptography
secp256k1 — the curve securing every Bitcoin private key

2²⁵⁶
Key space

secp256k1
Curve

The Math

Curve: y² = x³ + 7 over finite field Fp where p = 2²⁵⁶ − 2³² − 977

Generator point G is a specific point on the curve (hardcoded in the spec)

Private key k: random 256-bit integer (1 ≤ k < n, where n ≈ 2²⁵⁶)

Public key K = k × G — scalar multiplication of G by k (point addition repeated k times)

ECDSA Signing

To sign: generate random nonce r, compute R = r×G, s = r⁻¹(hash + k×Rx) mod n

Signature = (Rx mod n, s) — two 256-bit integers, 64 bytes total (DER-encoded: ~71 bytes)

Reusing nonce r for two different messages reveals private key k — PS3 hack exploit

Schnorr (BIP340): non-interactive signature aggregation, 64-byte fixed size, batch verification

Key Derivation — BIP32/44

HD wallets: master seed → master private key via HMAC-SHA512

Child key: HMAC-SHA512(parent_pubkey ∥ index) → child key + chain code

BIP44 path: m/44'/0'/0'/0/0 = purpose/coin/account/change/index

xpub (extended public key) lets you generate all receive addresses without exposing private keys
Mining Economics
The incentive structure that secures the Bitcoin network

3.125 BTC
Block reward

2028
Next halving

Block Reward

Current subsidy: 3.125 BTC per block (halved April 2024)

Halvings occur every 210,000 blocks (~4 years) — subsidy → 0 around year 2140

Fee revenue must eventually replace subsidy or miner incentives collapse

At $60K BTC, block reward = ~$187,500 — secures ~$15T network for ~$14B/year

Difficulty Adjustment

Retargets every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks) to maintain 10-min average block time

new_target = old_target × (actual_time / 1,209,600 seconds)

Capped at 4× change per adjustment period to prevent runaway difficulty swings

Current difficulty: ~88 trillion — 88T SHA-256² hashes needed per valid block on average

Mining Pools & ASICs

Solo mining: variance too high — expected block: years. Pools share rewards by hashrate contribution

Stratum protocol: pool sends simplified work units; miners report partial proofs (shares)

ASIC efficiency: ~30 J/TH — purpose-built chips 1000× more efficient than GPUs

Geographic distribution: ~65% US, ~10% Kazakhstan, ~8% Canada post-China ban
Altcoins & Token Standards
From Litecoin forks to ERC-20 DeFi tokens

$400B+
ERC-20

10+
NFT chains

Bitcoin Forks

Hard fork: protocol change requiring all nodes to upgrade — incompatible with old rules

Bitcoin Cash (2017): increased block size 8MB→32MB for more TPS — sacrifices decentralization

Bitcoin SV: further increased to 4GB blocks — nearly no adoption

Litecoin: 4× faster blocks (2.5 min), Scrypt PoW (ASIC-resistant initially), 84M supply cap

ERC-20 Standard

Interface: transfer(), approve(), transferFrom(), allowance(), balanceOf(), totalSupply()

Approve + transferFrom enables DEX trading without custody — basis of all DeFi

ERC-20 tokens live as entries in a smart contract's mapping(address → uint256)

ERC-777 (hooks), ERC-4626 (tokenized vaults), ERC-2612 (permit, gasless approvals) extend the standard

NFT Standards

ERC-721: non-fungible tokens — each tokenId is unique, ownerOf(tokenId) returns one address

ERC-1155: multi-token — one contract, multiple token types (fungible + NFT in same contract)

Metadata: tokenURI() returns JSON with name, image, attributes — usually IPFS or Arweave

Royalties: ERC-2981 on-chain royalty standard — marketplaces read and enforce automatically
On-Chain Analysis
Reading blockchain data to understand market dynamics

sell ratio
SOPR

network value/tx
NVT

UTXO Age Analysis

HODL waves: age-stratified UTXO distribution — old coins moving = potential sell pressure

SOPR (Spent Output Profit Ratio): avg profit ratio of coins spent today

SOPR > 1 = sellers in profit; SOPR = 1 = breakeven zone (often support in bull markets)

Realized cap: sum of each UTXO valued at price when last moved — smoother than market cap

Network Value Metrics

NVT (Network Value to Transactions): market cap / daily tx volume — high NVT = overvalued

MVRV (Market Value to Realized Value): market cap / realized cap — >3.5 historically = top signal

Stock-to-Flow: scarcity model based on existing supply / annual production — controversial for price prediction

Puell Multiple: daily miner revenue / 365-day avg — high = miners likely selling

Exchange Flow

Exchange inflow spike: coins moving to exchanges usually precede selling

Exchange reserves declining: coins leaving exchanges = long-term holding signal

Stablecoin supply ratio: stablecoin market cap / BTC market cap — more stables = more buying power

Whale wallet tracking: addresses with >1,000 BTC can be tracked for accumulation/distribution patterns
Wallets & Key Management
Hot, cold, hardware, and custodial options explained

12 or 24
Seed words

2048 words
BIP39

Wallet Types

Hot wallet: private key on internet-connected device — convenient, higher risk (MetaMask, Exodus)

Cold wallet: private key never online — hardware device (Ledger, Trezor) or paper wallet

Custodial: exchange holds keys on your behalf — 'not your keys, not your coins'

Multi-sig: M-of-N keys required to sign — 2-of-3 splits risk across devices/locations

BIP39 Mnemonic

128-256 bits of entropy + checksum → 12-24 words from 2048-word wordlist

Entropy → mnemonic → PBKDF2(mnemonic + passphrase, 'mnemonic', 2048 rounds) → 512-bit seed

From seed: BIP32 HD derivation → unlimited child keys for any coin/account/address

Passphrase (25th word) adds plausible deniability — different passphrase = different wallet

Security Best Practices

Never enter seed phrase online or in any software not specifically designed for it

Metal backup (Cryptosteel, Bilodeau) survives fire and flood — paper does not

Hardware wallet: private key never leaves device, transaction signed on-device

Air-gapped signing: PSBT (Partially Signed Bitcoin Transaction) passed via QR/USB between online+offline devices
Crypto Market Structure
Exchanges, liquidity, and price discovery mechanisms

$50B/day
CEX volume

$5B/day
DEX volume

Order Book Mechanics

Limit order: execute only at specified price or better — adds liquidity, earns maker rebate

Market order: execute immediately at best available price — takes liquidity, pays taker fee

Order book depth: bid/ask stacked by price level — thin book = high slippage for large orders

Market makers profit from bid-ask spread — typically 0.01-0.1% on liquid pairs

Price Discovery

Arbitrage keeps prices aligned across exchanges — bots exploit any gap within milliseconds

Funding rates (perpetuals): if longs > shorts, longs pay shorts funding (typically every 8h)

Premium/discount: spot vs futures price — contango = futures > spot (bullish bias), backwardation = bearish

Liquidation cascade: forced selling triggers more selling — amplifies volatility in leveraged markets

DEX vs CEX

CEX: fast matching, custody risk, KYC required, rebate programs for market makers

DEX (AMM): no custody, permissionless, 24/7, but higher gas costs and slippage on large orders

DEX aggregators (1inch, Paraswap): route across multiple AMMs for best execution price

Cross-chain bridges: enable assets to move between chains — historically highest exploit risk in DeFi
